entries.intro: There's a spot near {subject_focus_the} where sound doesn't work right. Stand in the middle of it and you can hear conversations from a mile away, clear as day.
details: It's a patch of flat ground between two ridges. About twenty paces across. Step outside the boundary and everything goes back to normal.
I stood there at dawn and heard the baker kneading bread in the village. Every slap of dough on the counter.
Voices from multiple directions overlap. You have to concentrate to pick out a single thread.
reactions: The herders discovered it years ago and kept it quiet. They use it to listen for predators near their flocks.
I'm not sure I like the idea of a place where secrets carry. Makes you think twice about what you say outdoors.

statCheck.pass: I tested the boundaries carefully. The listening zone is a perfect oval, oriented north-south. The ridges on either side have matching concave surfaces: they're acting like a pair of enormous ears, focusing sound to a point.
statCheck.fail: The edges seem to shift depending on the wind. I have tried to map it myself and could never pin down a consistent boundary.

entries.intro: The sundial near {subject_focus_the} has been telling the wrong time. Not drifting: it's exactly one hour ahead, and it's been that way for a month.
details: It was accurate for decades. The mason who built it knew his craft. Nothing about the dial has changed.
Other sundials in the area read correctly. It's just this one, and only since the last new moon.
reactions: The practical explanation is that something shifted the base. But I've checked: it hasn't moved a hair.
An hour ahead. Like it's seeing the sun in a position it won't reach for another sixty minutes. That's not how shadows work.

statCheck.pass: I brought a compass to the sundial and the needle spins: not a full rotation, just a wobble, about thirty degrees off true north. There's something under the ground here that's interfering with direction itself.
statCheck.fail: Everything on that sundial is precisely where it should be. The stone has not shifted, the angle has not changed. No obvious explanation.

entries.intro: A well near {subject_focus_the} has started echoing back words that nobody spoke. Drop a stone and instead of a splash, you hear a voice.
details: It's not a clear voice: more like a whisper, broken up by the echo. But the rhythm is speech, not water.
Different times of day give different responses. Morning whispers are calm. Evening ones sound urgent.
The water level hasn't changed. The well is functioning normally in every way except for the sounds it makes.
reactions: The children think it's ghosts. The adults think it's acoustics. I think both explanations are equally unsatisfying.
I lowered a lantern down the shaft to look. Everything seemed normal. But the whisper said something as I pulled the lantern back up. I couldn't make out the words.

statCheck.pass: I recorded the rhythm of the whispers over several days. They repeat: the same sequence of sounds every three days. It's not random chatter. It's a message on a loop.
statCheck.fail: The whispers are too faint and fractured to make out. It sounds like speech, but I cannot confirm it and neither can anyone else.

entries.intro: Compasses near {subject_focus_the} have been pointing to different norths depending on where you stand. Move ten paces and the needle swings twenty degrees.
details: It started about two weeks ago. Before that, every compass in the village agreed.
The worst spot is near the old foundation. Stand there and the needle spins slowly, never settling.
Travelers have been getting lost on the approach road because their instruments go unreliable within a mile of the village.
reactions: The cartographer is furious. He can't take accurate measurements for his survey work.
We've gone back to navigating by landmarks and sun position. It works, but it feels like a step backward.

entries.intro: There's a cave near {subject_focus_the} where your shadow doesn't follow you. You walk in and your shadow stays at the entrance.
details: I brought a torch and my shadow appeared on the wall behind me, pointing out. Not inward like it should. The light was in front of me, but my shadow fell toward it.
Other people's shadows do the same thing. Objects too: hold up a stick and its shadow points the wrong direction.
The cave is shallow: maybe thirty paces deep. Nothing unusual about the stone or the shape.
reactions: The children dare each other to go in. The adults generally don't. There's something deeply wrong about seeing your own shadow disobey.
A scholar visited and spent an hour inside with measuring tools. He left without saying anything and I haven't seen him since.
